result:
Depends on three things — answer each before calling it safe:

1. **Is the array bounded?** If cart size is user-controlled and can hit 100+, a single request can claim 20 connections and queue the rest, starving concurrent traffic. With even modest request concurrency (say 5 req/s × 30 SKUs), you'll exhaust the pool. Sequential loops have the opposite problem (slow) but don't starve.

2. **Is this inside a transaction?** If the original loop ran on a single checked-out connection (`BEGIN ... COMMIT`), `Promise.all` is **broken**, not just risky — you can't multiplex queries on one connection. Most drivers either serialize them silently (making the "parallelism" a lie) or throw. Check whether the surrounding handler uses `db.transaction(...)` or similar.

3. **What's the pool acquire timeout?** If it's short (pg default is no timeout; many setups set 5–30s), saturating the pool turns into request failures rather than slow requests. Sequential loops never saturate.

**The right fix is almost always neither**: replace N round-trips with one query.

```js
const rows = await db.query('SELECT sku, stock FROM inventory WHERE sku = ANY($1)', [skus]);
```

One connection, one round trip, no pool pressure, no concurrency cap to tune. Faster than both the old loop and the new `Promise.all`. If the inventory check has per-SKU side effects that can't be batched, bound concurrency with `p-limit`:

```js
const limit = pLimit(5);
await Promise.all(skus.map(sku => limit(() => checkSku(sku))));
```

Pick a cap well below pool max (e.g. 5) so a single request can't monopolize connections. Add an integration test that runs a large-cart order concurrently with other traffic against a real DB and asserts no `timeout acquiring connection` errors — this class of bug never surfaces in unit tests.
